items.title: The Sacred Spring: A Portrait of the Vienna Secession
items.subtitle: Art History
items.description: Imagine Vienna in the last years of the nineteenth century: a city drunk on its own reflection. The Ringstrasse glittered with palaces dressed in borrowed history – a Greek parliament here, a Gothic city hall there, a Renaissance opera in between. It was magnificent, and it was exhausted. Behind the gilt, an entire generation of young painters and architects had begun to suspect that their city was building museums to a past that had never quite belonged to it, while the present – electric, anxious, alive – went unpainted.

items.title: Five Rooms, One Signature
items.subtitle: Interior Design
items.description: A dalmatian dot, a slab of burl, three portraits of the same painted dog, and one small cone that keeps reappearing where you least expect it — the disciplined vocabulary one apartment uses five different ways...

items.title: The Threshold of Time
items.subtitle: Restoration
items.description: Door restoration is an act of archaeological tenderness. From the carved portals of Tbilisi to Ghiberti's Gates of Paradise — a journey through the cities that refuse to let their thresholds disappear.

items.title: Home Away From Home
items.subtitle: Design & Travel
items.description: Choosing a place to stay in a new destination means entrusting it with the significant moments of your life. Falling into bed after a long walk through unfamiliar streets, late-night talks filled with the thoughts and emotions of the day, planning the next breakfast at your newly favorite nearby boulangerie, sharing genuine smiles and the pure excitement of exploration.

articles.title: The Sacred Spring: A Portrait of the Vienna Secession
articles.author: Viacheslav Munister
articles.excerpt: In 1897 a handful of Viennese artists walked out on the establishment, raised a white temple crowned with a dome of gilded leaves, and declared to a startled city that every age deserves an art of its own. This is the story of the Secession – and of the restless, brilliant people who made it.

content.content: Imagine Vienna in the last years of the nineteenth century: a city drunk on its own reflection. The Ringstrasse glittered with palaces dressed in borrowed history – a Greek parliament here, a Gothic city hall there, a Renaissance opera in between. It was magnificent, and it was exhausted. Behind the gilt, an entire generation of young painters and architects had begun to suspect that their city was building museums to a past that had never quite belonged to it, while the present – electric, anxious, alive – went unpainted.

On the third of April 1897, a group of them stopped waiting for permission. Led by a thirty-four-year-old painter named Gustav Klimt, they resigned in a body from the Künstlerhaus, the official artists' association that controlled every important exhibition in the city. They called their new movement the Vereinigung bildender Künstler Österreichs – the Union of Austrian Artists – but history remembers them by the word they chose for the break itself: Secession.

They were not a school. They shared no single style, no common manifesto of technique. What bound them was a conviction – fierce, simple, and impossible to compromise – and they cut it in gold above the door of the building they would soon raise. It read: to every age its art, to art its freedom.
To Every Age Its Art

The Secession announced itself the way a thunderclap announces a storm – with a poster. For the first exhibition in 1898, Klimt drew Theseus slaying the Minotaur, the young hero of a new art putting the old monster to the sword, while Pallas Athene, goddess of wisdom and just war, stood guard at the margin. The censors made him add a screen of trees to hide Theseus's nakedness. It was the first of many quarrels the Secession would pick with a public that did not yet know it wanted to be provoked.
/images/secession/klimt-poster.jpg
content.caption: Gustav Klimt's poster for the first Secession exhibition, 1898. Theseus confronts the Minotaur; Athene stands watch at the right.

content.content: Their creed had a name too – Ver Sacrum, the 'sacred spring.' In ancient Italy it was the rite by which a people, in a time of crisis, dedicated the coming generation to a fresh start. The Secessionists borrowed the phrase for their magazine, a square, exquisite journal in which text and image were designed as one living surface. To open an issue of Ver Sacrum was to hold the movement's philosophy in your hands: that a wine glass, a typeface, a chair and a painting were all art, and all equally worth perfecting.
/images/secession/versacrum.jpg
content.caption: The first issue of Ver Sacrum, January 1898 – the movement's magazine, and its manifesto in print.

content.content: Der Zeit ihre Kunst. Der Kunst ihre Freiheit. – To every age its art. To art its freedom.
The White Temple

A movement needs a home, and the Secession built one unlike anything Vienna had seen. The commission went to Joseph Maria Olbrich, barely thirty, a pupil of the great Otto Wagner. What he produced in 1898 was less a building than a declaration: a cubic, white-walled temple of pure geometry, stripped of every historical ornament, crowned with an openwork dome of three thousand gilded laurel leaves. Vienna, half-scandalised and half-delighted, nicknamed it 'the golden cabbage.'

Look closely at the façade and you find the movement's spirit written in plaster: three solemn owls, sacred to Athene, and above the entrance those defiant golden words. Even the decorative details were designed by members of the group – the owls and foliage by Koloman Moser – so that the building itself became a total work of art, a Gesamtkunstwerk, in which architecture, sculpture and ornament spoke a single language.
/images/secession/hero.jpg
content.caption: Koloman Moser's owls and laurel on the Secession façade – Athene's birds, guarding a temple of new art.

content.content: The People

The Secession was never a one-man movement, but it began, unmistakably, with one man. Gustav Klimt was the son of a gold engraver, and gold would never leave his work. He had made his name – and his fortune – painting decorous allegories on the ceilings of the very Ringstrasse palaces the Secession would rebel against. Then, at the height of his respectability, he turned. His art grew strange, erotic, ornamental and unafraid; his patrons grew nervous; and Klimt, serenely, kept going.
/images/secession/klimt-portrait.jpg
content.caption: Gustav Klimt in his painter's smock, photographed in 1914. The first president of the Secession.

content.content: In Pallas Athene of 1898 you can already see the future arriving. The goddess is armoured in hammered gold, her gaze cool and frontal, and in her hand she holds a tiny naked figure – Nuda Veritas, naked truth, holding up a mirror to a society that preferred not to look. It was Klimt's private motto made visible: the artist owes the age not flattery, but honesty.
/images/secession/pallas.jpg
content.caption: Klimt, Pallas Athene, 1898 – the goddess in beaten gold, holding naked Truth in her palm.

content.content: If Klimt was the movement's heart, Otto Wagner was its conscience turned to stone and iron. Already famous, already past fifty, the professor could have coasted into a comfortable old age of neo-Baroque commissions. Instead he threw in his lot with his own students, declaring that 'something impractical cannot be beautiful' and that the architecture of a new age must be born of new life, new materials, new needs.
/images/secession/wagner-portrait.jpg
content.caption: Otto Wagner – the elder master who chose the side of the young.

content.content: Wagner's buildings still stun. On the Linke Wienzeile he sheathed an entire apartment house in blossoming majolica tiles, flowers climbing five storeys into the Viennese sky – practical, weatherproof, and gloriously alive. And at Karlsplatz he built a pair of railway pavilions so elegant that the city's commuters passed each day through works of art, green iron and white marble stitched with golden sunflowers.
/images/secession/wagner-majolica.jpg
content.caption: Wagner's Majolica House (1898) – an apartment block dressed in flowering ceramic tiles.

content.content: /images/secession/wagner-station.jpg
content.caption: The Karlsplatz Stadtbahn pavilion (1898) – a railway station as a jewel of green and gold.

content.content: Koloman Moser was the movement's quiet universe. Trained as a painter, he designed everything: postage stamps, stained glass, book covers, furniture, the owls on the Secession wall, the very pages of Ver Sacrum. If the Secession believed that art should saturate ordinary life, Moser was the proof – a man who could make a letterbox as beautiful as an altarpiece.
/images/secession/moser.jpg
content.caption: Koloman Moser – painter, graphic genius, and the man who designed almost everything.

content.content: Josef Hoffmann took that belief and built an empire on it. In 1903, with Moser and the banker Fritz Wärndorfer, he founded the Wiener Werkstätte – the Vienna Workshops – a cooperative of craftsmen determined to make objects of total quality, from cutlery to entire houses. His grids and squares were so rigorous that colleagues nicknamed him 'Quadratl-Hoffmann,' Little-Square Hoffmann. The nickname was a joke; the influence was not. His purity of line runs straight through the twentieth century into the design language we still live inside.
/images/secession/hoffmann.jpg
content.caption: Josef Hoffmann – co-founder of the Wiener Werkstätte, apostle of the perfect square.

content.content: And Joseph Maria Olbrich, the young architect of the temple itself, gave the movement its face. He did not stay in Vienna long – the Grand Duke of Hesse called him to Darmstadt to build an entire artists' colony from the ground up – but the golden dome he left behind still crowns the city, and still keeps its promise, a century and a quarter later.
/images/secession/olbrich.jpg
content.caption: Joseph Maria Olbrich – the architect who was barely thirty when he built the Secession's temple.

content.content: The Beethoven Exhibition

Everything the Secession believed came together, once, in a single room. In 1902 the fourteenth exhibition was conceived as a temple within the temple: a shrine to Ludwig van Beethoven, the artist as hero and prophet. At its centre stood Max Klinger's polychrome statue of the composer enthroned like a god; around the walls, twenty-one members contributed works meant to be seen together, as a single act of devotion. And along three walls Klimt painted the Beethoven Frieze – thirty-four metres of longing, suffering and deliverance.

It begins with the yearning of humankind, drifts through the temptations and terrors of the world – the giant Typhoeus, a monstrous ape, and the Gorgons of sickness, madness and death – and resolves, at last, in art and love. The frieze was painted directly on the wall, meant to be destroyed when the show closed. It survived by accident, and today it is one of the supreme achievements of European painting.
/images/secession/beethoven-frieze.jpg
content.caption: The Beethoven Frieze, 1902 – 'The Hostile Powers': the giant Typhoeus and the Gorgons of sickness, madness and death.

content.content: But the frieze does not end in darkness. It climbs toward a choir of angels and closes on an embrace – a naked couple locked together beneath a golden sky, and above them the words of Schiller that Beethoven set to music in his Ninth Symphony: this kiss to the whole world. It is the Secession's deepest belief, painted large: that art, in the end, is a form of love, and love a form of deliverance.
/images/secession/beethoven-kiss.jpg
content.caption: The frieze's finale – the choir of paradise and the embrace: 'This kiss to the whole world.'

content.content: The Break, and the Gold

Movements are mortal, and the Secession's unity did not survive its success. By 1905 a rift had opened between the 'stylists' around Klimt, who wanted art and craft and commerce to fuse completely, and the 'naturalists' who feared the movement was becoming a shop. Klimt and his circle – Moser, Hoffmann, Wagner among them – resigned. The group they had founded eight years earlier went on without them; so, gloriously, did they.

For it was in the years just after the break that Klimt entered his golden phase, and painted the picture that would eclipse everything else: The Kiss. Two figures kneel at the edge of a flowering meadow, wrapped in a single robe of shimmering gold, the man's patterns hard and rectangular, the woman's soft and circular, the whole world dissolved into ornament around them. Everything the Secession had argued for – the union of painting and decoration, the fearlessness before beauty, the gold inherited from a father's workshop – arrives here, complete.
/images/secession/klimt-kiss.jpg
content.caption: Gustav Klimt, The Kiss, 1907–1908 – the golden summit of everything the Secession set in motion.

content.content: The Secession Building still stands on the Friedrichstrasse, its dome still gilded, the Beethoven Frieze still glowing in the basement below. The words above the door have not changed in a hundred and twenty-eight years: to every age its art, to art its freedom. It remains, perhaps, the finest thing a group of young artists ever dared to promise – and one of the very few such promises that the world went on to keep.

articles.title: The Heart of EPRIS: Maria Is Now a Bachelor of Architecture
articles.author: EPRIS Journal
articles.excerpt: On July 7th, Mariia Ivanova, EPRIS's editor-in-chief, officially became a Bachelor of Architecture. To mark the day – a heart built from the architecture and art that inspire her.

content.content: Yesterday, on July 7th, 2026, something special happened in the life of the EPRIS editorial team. Mariia Ivanova – our editor-in-chief, and the person whose brainchild this journal is – officially became a Bachelor of Architecture. Years of drafting, models, and sleepless nights before her thesis defense are behind her now. A new chapter begins – and we couldn't let it pass without marking it here, on the pages of EPRIS.
It's no secret that an architect's eye runs through every issue of EPRIS – in the way we talk about space, light, and detail. Today, that eye earned its official credentials.

Those who worked alongside her on this issue know the ritual well: studio lights on late into the night, tracing paper covered in revisions, a cup of coffee gone cold beside the model she kept rebuilding until it finally felt right. That same restlessness – the refusal to settle for ‘good enough’ – is what has shaped this magazine since its very first page.
A Heart Made of What Inspired Her

content.caption: Forty-seven places and works of art that have shaped the way Mariia sees the world – from the Sagrada Família to Michelangelo's David. A little of each of them finds its way into every issue of EPRIS.

content.content: Some of these buildings she has visited in person, sketchbook in hand. Others she has only ever seen in photographs. We hope that one day, she gets to see every single one of them with her own eyes.
Architecture taught me to see the structure behind the beauty. EPRIS taught me to tell others about it. To me, they're two languages for the same feeling.

The EPRIS editorial team congratulates Mariia on her degree, and thanks her for the inspiration that lives in every issue of the journal. This diploma marks a new chapter – and we suspect it won't be the last one worth celebrating. We're glad you're reading it with us.

articles.title: Five Rooms, One Signature
articles.author: EPRIS Journal
articles.excerpt: A dalmatian dot, a slab of burl, three portraits of the same painted dog, and one small cone that keeps reappearing where you least expect it — the disciplined vocabulary one apartment uses five different ways without ever repeating itself.

content.content: Walk through this apartment once and you'll clock the polka dot, the burl veneer, the pair of painted dogs watching from three different walls. Walk through it twice and you'll notice none of them ever appears the same way twice — the dot changes scale on every surface it lands on, the veneer goes from tabletop to cabinet front to a single framed square the size of a postcard, and the dogs shift from grey to black-and-white to a single cropped blue portrait, as if the same idea keeps being translated rather than repeated. That's the real subject here: not a look, but a small, disciplined set of decisions a home can apparently sustain across five very different rooms without ever repeating itself outright.
https://api.eprisjournal.com/uploads/mrgg0dfmpqmyrg.jpg
content.caption: The dot at its largest scale: a full sectional, upholstered edge to edge, the pattern reading almost as texture before it reads as pattern.

content.content: Up close, this is not a printed polka dot — it's drawn, each mark an irregular blot with its own weight and edge, the kind of variation that happens when a hand repeats a gesture rather than a machine repeating a die-cut. At sofa scale that irregularity does real work: a perfectly regular dot this large would read as upholstery fabric behaving like wallpaper, decorative and slightly cheap. An irregular one reads as texture — closer to the fur it's clearly quoting than to a print. The skirted coffee table underneath, in the same dot at a smaller, denser scale, is the one piece of furniture in the room dressed rather than upholstered — a wood disc on a fabric petticoat, a deliberately strange thing to set next to a sectional this architectural. A small metal cone sits on that table next to a stack of books — the same silhouette the bedroom lamp repeats later, at a completely different scale, in a completely different material, though nothing about this room announces that yet.
The same idea, translated five times, is a collection. The same idea, copied five times, is a theme.
https://api.eprisjournal.com/uploads/mrgg0dpaojhrg2.jpg
content.caption: The same burl veneer three ways in one shot: a low credenza, a floating shelf, a single framed square holding a photograph instead of a view.

content.content: Burl is wood at its least geometric — the pattern comes from a tree's stress response, a knot it tried and failed to grow around, so no two panels of it can ever be book-matched the way straight-grain veneer can. Using that much of it, at this many scales, only works because everything touching it is so plain: a clean-lined credenza, a cantilevered shelf with no visible bracket, a floral armchair with soft, boneless-looking arms. That chair is the one curved, patterned object let into a room otherwise built from straight edges and one wild, unrepeatable grain — the same trick the polka dot pulls next to the burl nightstand in the bedroom, just in a different material. A second cone turns up here too, catching the light on the same low round table — small enough to miss, insistent enough that once you've seen it once, you start looking for it everywhere else in the apartment.
Repeat an object enough times, in different sizes and different materials, and it stops being decoration and starts being a signature.
https://api.eprisjournal.com/uploads/mrgg0dj90g3ij9.jpg
content.caption: A third dog, a third register: black and white instead of blue-grey, posed instead of caught mid-turn, hung above a burl console instead of a wood-panelled wall.

content.content: Three paintings of the same subject, three different colour treatments, and not one of them hung as a matching set — that's the tell that this is a collection built by eye, not ordered as a triptych. The chairs here do the same quiet work as the sofa's dot: a floral fabric, blue on white, wrapped around a frame with almost no right angles in it, so the pattern reads as movement rather than upholstery. Underneath, the same burl slab from the living room reappears as a table edge, thick enough to read as solid wood rather than veneer on plywood — the one place in the apartment where the material gets to be the whole object instead of a surface on top of one. The table's legs are polished to a full mirror, which does something the burl can't: it doubles the room instead of just decorating it, folding the chairs and their floral fabric back into the reflection like a second, slightly bluer dining room underneath the real one.
https://api.eprisjournal.com/uploads/mrgg0dn3pl70qq.jpg
content.caption: The dot at bedding scale, denser and smaller again, with the curtain behind it as a fourth scale still.

content.content: This is the same fabric logic as the sofa, run through a full range of scales in a single room: one dot on the curtain, a different-weight dot on the coverlet, a third scale entirely in the woven stripe underneath it, and a fourth blue-toned dog watching over all of it from a single cropped portrait rather than a full pose. Nothing here is trying to match — the curtain's dot and the bedding's dot are close enough to belong to the same family and different enough that the eye keeps working to place them, which is most of why the room reads as layered instead of themed. Pull back and the bed itself is doing the same layering trick as the room around it: a ribbed brown blanket, a striped waffle throw, and the dot coverlet on top, three different textures stacked the way the three dog portraits are stacked across three different rooms — repetition disguised as variety, or maybe the other way around.
https://api.eprisjournal.com/uploads/mrgg0dhfp07cpi.jpg
content.caption: The one corner of the apartment where the whole vocabulary collapses into a single, small composition: dot, burl, wood, one deliberate diagonal.

content.content: A cone tapers to a point with no seam, generated by nothing more than a straight line spun around an axis. Underneath it, a hexagon built from two stacked prisms, six flat planes each, closing on itself because six is the only number under ten that divides 360 without a remainder. One shape has no edges at all; the other is almost nothing but edges — and setting the cone's point directly above the hexagon's centre is what lets two unrelated families of geometry read as a single object. Behind both, the dot grid is the strictest geometry in the apartment, fixed interval, no dot more important than its neighbour — except that up close every mark is drawn, not stamped, the same disciplined irregularity holding the whole home together at every scale it appears. By the time the cone reaches this nightstand, it has already appeared twice as a small object on two different coffee tables — which means the lamp isn't introducing a new shape into the apartment so much as finally giving one of its recurring shapes somewhere serious to stand. The lamp is the one line in the frame that refuses the grid: its shade tips toward the pillow at almost exactly the angle a person leans in to say one last thing before a room goes dark — the single object here allowed to behave like it has somewhere to be.
Three coffee-table cones and one lamp aren't a coincidence. They're the closest thing this apartment has to a signature.

None of this explains why the apartment feels calm rather than styled — restraint does that, and so does resisting the urge to match anything exactly. But counting how many times the same dot, the same burl, the same cone, and the same painted dog show up in five rooms, at five different scales, is a decent way to start noticing why some homes read as collected and most just read as decorated.

articles.title: The Threshold of Time
articles.author: Viacheslav Munister
articles.excerpt: Door restoration is an act of archaeological tenderness. From the carved portals of Tbilisi to Ghiberti's Gates of Paradise — a journey through the cities that refuse to let their thresholds disappear.

content.content: There is a peculiar kind of silence that inhabits old doorways — a silence woven from centuries of hands pressing against timber, of keys turning in iron locks worn smooth by ten thousand evenings. Stand before a weathered portal in Lisbon's Alfama, and you can feel it: the grain of the wood holds the oils of five generations of palms, the hinges remember the rhythm of a family's comings and goings, and the threshold stone — scooped concave by two hundred years of footsteps — maps the topology of human habit more faithfully than any census. A door is never merely an architectural element. It is the most intimate surface a building offers to the street, the frontier between the public and the private, the place where a city's soul greets its visitors. To restore a door is to perform surgery on the memory of a place.

Door restoration is not merely carpentry. It is an act of archaeological tenderness, a conversation between the living and the vanished. When a conservator peels away layers of paint — cerulean blue over terracotta over bone white — they are performing stratigraphic analysis, reading the autobiography of a building one pigment at a time. Each colour corresponds to a decade, each crack charts a winter survived, each replaced hinge marks a family that once named this threshold home. The paint cross-section, examined under a stereomicroscope at 40× magnification, reveals not just hues but binders: the linseed-oil grounds of the eighteenth century, the lead-white primers of the Napoleonic era, the alkyd enamels of the mid-twentieth century, each layer a forensic timestamp more precise than any archive.

A single historic door can harbour up to twelve discrete paint strata. In Saint Petersburg, conservators from the 'Dveri s Pomoek' collective have documented pre-revolutionary pigments — Prussian blue, chrome yellow, genuine vermilion — entombed beneath Soviet-era PF-115 enamel. Some of these pigments had not absorbed a photon of visible light for over a century before the scalpel exposed them.

The fundamental tension in door conservation sits between two imperatives that the uninitiated often confuse: restoration and conservation. Restoration implies returning an object to a specific historical state, which demands interpretive choices — which century's appearance do you privilege? Conservation, by contrast, aims to stabilise the object in its current condition, arresting decay without erasing the patina of time. The modern consensus, codified in the Venice Charter of 1964 and refined by the Burra Charter of 1979, leans decisively toward conservation: intervene minimally, document exhaustively, and ensure reversibility. Every epoxy fill, every dutchman splice, every re-gilded surface must be distinguishable from the original upon close inspection, and removable without damaging the host material.

The door is the first sentence the building speaks to the street. Destroy it, and you erase the opening line of a story that took centuries to compose. Replace it with a replica, and you have written fan fiction in the margins of a manuscript.

Consider the materiality of a Pombaline door in Lisbon's Baixa district. After the catastrophic earthquake of November 1, 1755 — which killed between 30,000 and 50,000 people and levelled 85% of the city — the Marquis of Pombal commissioned a radical reconstruction based on proto-seismic engineering. The resulting Pombaline architecture introduced the 'gaiola pombalina' — a flexible timber-frame cage embedded within masonry walls, designed to flex rather than fracture during tremors. The doors of these buildings were crafted from Brazilian peroba rosa (Aspidosperma polyneuron) and pau-marfim (Balfourodendron riedelianum), imported from the Atlantic colonies: dense, resinous hardwoods with an interlocking grain that resists both marine humidity and the torsional stress of seismic movement. The joinery is mortise-and-tenon throughout, with wedged through-tenons at the stile-and-rail junctions — no nails, no screws, only the geometry of wood locking against wood.
https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1760468710389-1023382b2aed?w=900&h=600&fit=crop
content.caption: A weathered doorway in Lisbon's Alfama district — centuries of Atlantic wind, salt crystallisation, and ultraviolet degradation etched into every tangential grain.

content.content: Restoring a Pombaline door begins with a condition survey. The conservator taps the panels with a small brass mallet, listening for the dull thud of sound wood versus the hollow resonance of internal rot. Moisture content is measured with a pin-type meter: readings above 20% indicate active fungal colonisation, typically Serpula lacrymans (dry rot) or Coniophora puteana (cellar fungus). Insect damage — the sinuous galleries of Anobium punctatum (common furniture beetle) or, in tropical hardwoods, the broader tunnels of Lyctus brunneus (powder-post beetle) — is mapped with a dental probe. Only after this forensic inventory does physical intervention begin.

The treatment protocol follows a strict hierarchy. First, consolidation: a low-viscosity epoxy resin (typically a two-part system with a 3:1 ratio of resin to hardener, viscosity below 500 centipoise) is injected into delaminated panels and decayed end-grain through 2mm drill holes, penetrating the cellular structure by capillary action. The epoxy cross-links within the wood fibres, creating a composite material that retains the original surface texture while recovering structural integrity. For more extensive losses, the conservator cuts a 'dutchman' — a precisely fitted wooden insert, grain-matched to the surrounding material, bonded with a reversible PVA adhesive rather than an irreversible epoxy, so that future conservators can remove and replace it without collateral damage.

Hardware receives parallel attention. The wrought-iron strap hinges of a seventeenth-century door were not mass-produced but hand-forged: each pintle was drawn from a single bloom of smelted ore, the surface bearing the distinctive hammer-scale texture of a charcoal forge operating at approximately 1,100°C. When corroded, these elements are stabilised with a tannic acid conversion treatment — the tannins react with the iron(III) oxide to form iron(III) tannate, a stable, blue-black corrosion-resistant layer — rather than sandblasted, which would erase the forging marks that constitute the object's technological signature. Missing nails are reproduced using traditional heading tools: a nail rod is drawn through a swage plate, headed in a nail header, and given the characteristic rosehead profile by four blows of a ball-pein hammer, replicating a seventeenth-century technique documented in Moxon's 'Mechanick Exercises' of 1677.

content.caption: Six thresholds, six cities: from Lisbon's azulejo-framed entries to Saint Petersburg's imperial doorways.

content.content: Travel eastward, and the doors of Tbilisi reveal an entirely different grammar of beauty — one rooted not in post-catastrophe rationalism but in the exuberant decorative traditions of the Caucasus. In the labyrinthine streets of the Old Town, Art Nouveau doors with oval stained-glass windows in lead came coexist beside austere Georgian carved panels depicting grapevines and mythical beasts, executed in local walnut (Juglans regia) with chip-carving techniques unchanged since the Middle Ages. The celebrated Kaleidoscope entrance at 3 Betlemi Street — known locally as 'Gallery 27' — scatters Caucasian sunlight through a mosaic of coloured glass into a hundred chromatic whispers on the stairwell walls. The Shalimov House at 13 Lado Asatiani Street still guards its angel frescoes above a threshold where, legend maintains, the poet Nikoloz Baratashvili once paused to compose a verse on the nature of longing.

The Tbilisi Development Fund's 'New Tbilisi' project has systematically restored historic entrances on Atoneli Street since 2020, at a documented cost of 414,886 Georgian Lari per pair of entrances. The scope of work reads like a surgical protocol: restoration of marble floor and stair treads, conservation of wooden doors and windows (including re-glazing with period-appropriate crown glass), casting and installation of missing cast-iron balustrade details from silicone moulds taken from surviving originals, and — most remarkably — the uncovering of forgotten wall paintings sealed behind plaster for decades. At 31 Atoneli Street, workers removing a layer of Soviet-era cement render discovered a polychrome mural depicting pastoral scenes, its original casein binder still viable, the pigments — earth ochres, ultramarine, and bone black — as saturated as the day they were applied.
https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1617372155383-e53c256df29a?w=900&h=600&fit=crop
content.caption: Carved wooden balconies and painted doors in Tbilisi's Old Town — each doorway a palimpsest of Georgian, Persian, and Soviet layers.

content.content: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/59/Lorenzo_ghiberti%2C_porta_del_paradiso%2C_1425-52%2C_00.JPG
content.caption: Monumental bronze doors of Florence — where Nd:YAG laser ablation meets mercury-fire gilding.

content.content: Florence offers the most technically documented example of door restoration as high art. Lorenzo Ghiberti's Porta del Paradiso — the gilded bronze Gates of Paradise at the Baptistery of San Giovanni — underwent a twenty-seven-year conservation at the Opificio delle Pietre Dure that concluded in 2012. The challenge was formidable: five centuries of atmospheric corrosion had deposited a crust of copper carbonates (malachite and azurite), sulphates (brochantite), and chlorides (atacamite) over the original mercury-fire gilding — a technique in which gold leaf is amalgamated with mercury at approximately 350°C and applied to the bronze surface, the mercury then driven off by controlled heating, leaving a microscopically thin but extraordinarily durable gold film.

The restoration team, led by conservators Annamaria Giusti and Mauro Matteini, developed a bespoke Nd:YAG laser cleaning protocol. The laser, operating at 1064 nanometres wavelength with pulse durations of 8 nanoseconds, selectively ablated the dark corrosion products through differential absorption: the dark crust absorbed the infrared radiation and vaporised, while the underlying gold — highly reflective at this wavelength — remained unaffected. Thermal modelling ensured that the instantaneous temperature rise in the gold film never exceeded 50°C, far below the threshold that would cause micro-cracking or delamination. Where the corrosion was too tenacious for laser alone, controlled chemical poultices of EDTA (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid) at 5% concentration in deionised water were applied through Japanese tissue paper for precisely timed intervals, chelating the copper ions without attacking the substrate. The result: each of the ten panels, depicting scenes from Genesis to the story of Solomon, emerged with the luminous splendour that Michelangelo himself praised when he named these doors worthy of Paradise.

Speed is irrelevant when you are restoring something that took three generations to build. The Nd:YAG laser removes corrosion at four square centimetres per hour. Ghiberti spent twenty-seven years casting these doors. We spent twenty-seven years cleaning them. The symmetry is not accidental — it is respect.

Saint Petersburg — that northernmost Venice of granite and fog — holds a singular position in the cartography of endangered doors. The pre-revolutionary entrances of the Petrogradskaya Side, with their linden-wood (Tilia cordata) panels and wrought-iron transom grilles, represent some of the most imperilled specimens of urban ornament in Europe. The volunteer collective 'Dveri s Pomoek' — 'Doors from the Dump' — was founded by engineers Valentina Mann and Andrei Troshkov and designer Alexander Artemyev, who met at the Tom Sawyer Fest, a grassroots festival of historic building restoration that originated in Samara in 2015 and has since spread to over sixty Russian cities.

Their methodology is forensic. When a discarded door is retrieved — often from a skip outside a building undergoing 'renovation' by developers who consider plastic replacements an upgrade — it is first photographed in situ, then transported to the workshop for assessment. The species is identified: Karelian birch (Betula pendula var. carelica) with its distinctive flame figuring, Scots pine (Pinus sylvestris) from the Archangelsk forests, or Siberian larch (Larix sibirica), the same timber used in Venetian pilings and prized for its extraordinary resistance to moisture. Paint stratigraphy is performed on a micro-core sample: each layer is catalogued against known periods of building maintenance, cross-referenced with the building's archival records when available. The restorers have documented doors with hand-painted floral cartouches — executed in casein-bound distemper over a gesso ground — that survived beneath layers of Soviet khaki enamel, the delicate petals still discernible despite decades of chemical and mechanical insult.
https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1723027094512-60b950f0d292?w=900&h=600&fit=crop
content.caption: A Saint Petersburg courtyard entrance — behind these gates, volunteers of Tom Sawyer Fest salvage pre-revolutionary doors from demolition.

content.content: Mann notes a grim irony: 'The best-preserved doors are often the ones the Soviets painted over most aggressively. The very enamel that obliterated their beauty also sealed them from oxygen and UV, preserving the original pigments in a state that borders on anaerobic conservation. When we peel back the PF-115, we find colours that a museum conservator would envy.' The collective's restoration of linden-wood doors from the Kamienny Ostrov area required eleven months of patient work: chemical paint removal using NMP (N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone) — chosen over methylene chloride for its lower toxicity and slower evaporation rate, which allows for more controlled stripping — followed by consolidation of worm-damaged areas with Paraloid B-72 in acetone at 5% concentration, and final finishing with cold-pressed linseed oil, applied in four thin coats and allowed to cure for seventy-two hours between applications.
Durham Cathedral, England

At Durham Cathedral in northern England, the North Door presents a conservation challenge of an entirely different order: how to preserve a structure that has been in continuous functional use for nearly nine centuries. Dendrochronological analysis of the oak (Quercus robur) places the felling date of the timber between 1109 and 1144, making it contemporary with the cathedral's original Romanesque construction. The door is not a museum piece behind glass — it is opened and closed daily by tourists, choristers, and clergy, its iron ring handle worn to a mirror finish by 900 years of human touch.

The 2019 conservation by Skillingtons Workshop exemplified the minimalist philosophy that defines contemporary best practice. Phase one: dry-brushing with natural-bristle brushes and low-suction vacuuming to remove trapped particulate matter from the open grain — medieval oak, unlike modern kiln-dried timber, retains a pronounced tangential grain pattern that acts as a trap for atmospheric soot and mineral dust. Phase two: aqueous cleaning with de-ionised water and a non-ionic surfactant (Synperonic A7) at 1% concentration, tested first on a concealed section of the rebate to verify that no dimensional change or surface blanching occurred. Phase three: re-saturation with cold-pressed Tung oil (Vernicia fordii), applied in four successive coats over sixteen days, each coat absorbed before the next was brushed on. Tung oil was chosen over linseed for its superior resistance to hydrolysis and its lower tendency to yellow with age. Phase four: the corroded wrought-iron fittings were stabilised with Renaissance Wax — a microcrystalline blend developed by the British Museum in the 1950s — rather than varnish, which would have created a non-breathing barrier antithetical to the iron's long-term preservation.
https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1760382739146-99c865bd4152?w=900&h=600&fit=crop
content.caption: Durham Cathedral — nine centuries of English oak, iron, and faith embedded in its Romanesque arches.

content.content: In Havana, the challenge is not preservation of the ancient but resurrection of the abandoned. The crumbling pastel facades of Centro Habana conceal portals of Cuban mahogany (Swietenia mahagoni) and Spanish cedar (Cedrela odorata) — aromatic, naturally insect-resistant hardwoods that once welcomed sugar barons and revolutionary poets alike. The tropical climate imposes unique stresses: relative humidity regularly exceeds 85%, driving cycles of expansion and contraction that no temperate-climate joinery was designed to withstand. Cuban restorers — many trained at the Oficina del Historiador de la Ciudad, the heritage office established by Eusebio Leal Spengler — work with tools inherited from their grandfathers: wooden moulding planes with hand-forged blades, bow saws strung with tempered wire, and scratch stocks for reproducing ogee and cyma recta profiles. They re-carve rosettes and acanthus leaves not from measured drawings, which dissolved long ago in the tropical humidity, but from plaster casts taken from surviving examples in neighbouring buildings — a technique that preserves the local 'dialect' of ornamental carving, the subtle variations that distinguish Havana Baroque from its Andalusian ancestors.
https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1777518120419-24a9b9650b0d?w=900&h=600&fit=crop
content.caption: A Cuban doorway where mahogany meets maritime humidity — the weathered blue paint a century's palimpsest of Caribbean salt and sun.

content.content: Prague's doors speak in the dialect of Central European Baroque: a language of carved limewood (Tilia europaea) featuring saints, griffins, and garlands of impossible fruit rendered with a virtuosity that rivals the altarpiece carvings of Tilman Riemenschneider. In Malá Strana, beneath the shadow of Prague Castle, the portals of aristocratic palaces bear the heraldic devices of families who have been extinct for three centuries, their carved escutcheons the last material evidence that these lineages ever existed. The Palazzo Negri in Istrian Vodnjan — restored by the Ars et Labor Foundation during the European Year of Cultural Heritage in 2018 — demonstrated how a single Didactic Workshop, staffed by volunteer conservators and funded entirely by private donors, can preserve a region's identity one door at a time. The project's ten-month timeline included complete dismantling of the Baroque door, treatment of each component, and reassembly using traditional hide-glue joinery. Their motto, borrowed from the Gospel of Matthew, is quietly magnificent: 'Knock, and the door will be opened to you.'
https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1757004953637-9cb42bbda6c8?w=900&h=600&fit=crop
content.caption: Ornate church entrance in Prague — where Gothic tracery meets baroque wood carving in the lanes of Malá Strana.

content.content: The economics of door conservation are rarely discussed, yet they are decisive. A custom-fabricated fiberglass replacement door for a historic building costs between €800 and €2,000; a sympathetic conservation of the original, including paint analysis, timber repair, and hardware stabilisation, typically runs €3,000 to €8,000, depending on condition and complexity. The difference is not trivial, and developers routinely invoke it to justify replacement. But the calculus is incomplete. The original door embodies what economists call 'embedded energy' — the sum of human labour, material cultivation, transportation, and craft that produced it. A linden door from 1890s Saint Petersburg required a tree that germinated during the reign of Catherine the Great; its timber was air-dried for two to five years before a master joiner shaped it. No budget line captures this temporal investment. No replacement, however skilful, replicates it.

items: Photograph every elevation, profile, and hardware detail before any intervention
Extract a micro-core paint sample for stratigraphic and binding-medium analysis
Measure moisture content with a pin-type meter at three depths: surface, mid-panel, and core
Probe for insect galleries using a dental explorer; map damage on a scaled elevation drawing
Test all cleaning agents on a concealed area and observe for 72 hours before proceeding
Replace only structurally failed material — use dutchman splices with grain-matched timber
Bond repairs with reversible adhesives (PVA or Paraloid B-72), never irreversible epoxies on surfaces
Reproduce missing hardware using period-appropriate techniques: hand-forging, not casting
Apply finish coats (linseed or Tung oil) in thin layers with full cure time between each
Compile a conservation dossier: photographs, material analysis, intervention log, for future custodians

content.caption: The Restorer's Decalogue: Ten Principles of Ethical Door Conservation

content.content: There is a Japanese concept — kintsugi — that repairs broken ceramics with lacquer mixed with powdered gold, treating the fracture not as a flaw to be concealed but as a chapter in the object's biography. The best door conservation operates on a parallel philosophy. The 'dutchman' insert is not hidden; it is grain-matched but left legible, a frank acknowledgment that repair has occurred. The tannic-acid-blackened hinge does not pretend to be unworn; it wears its patina as evidence of nine centuries of openings and closings. The conservation report, archived alongside the door, ensures that the next conservator — in fifty years, in a hundred — will know exactly what was done, and why, and by whom. This is the opposite of vanity renovation. This is humility encoded in wood and iron.

content.question: Which city's historic doors fascinate you most?

content.content: What makes a door worth saving? Not merely the age of the timber or the elegance of the carving — though both matter. It is the accumulated weight of passage: the thousands of arrivals and departures, the lovers who lingered in the frame, the children who scratched their heights into the jamb, the mourners who paused before crossing back into sunlight. Every threshold is a palimpsest of human experience, layered as densely as the paint stratigraphy on a Pombaline panel. When we permit a developer to rip out a nineteenth-century entrance and replace it with a pressed steel door from a catalogue, we do not merely lose an object. We erase a sentence from the city's autobiography — and unlike a book, a city cannot be reprinted.

In the end, the conservator's craft is an act of radical empathy extended across time. To sand a piece of oak that a Romanesque carpenter shaped nine centuries ago is to shake hands with the dead. To mix pigments that match a Baroque painter's palette is to complete a conversation interrupted by centuries. Every door that survives is a small victory over entropy, a proof that beauty can outlast the indifference of progress. The threshold of time stands open. Our choice — the only choice that matters — is whether we walk through it with the reverence it deserves, or seal it shut with the concrete of forgetfulness.
Explore the Ars et Labor Foundation's 'Knock and the Door Will Be Opened' Project

articles.title: Home Away From Home
articles.author: Maria Ivanova
articles.excerpt: A pied-à-terre always catches the irreplaceable light in your eyes, but it can also spark them.

content.content: /images/article-img-1.png

Choosing a place to stay in a new destination means entrusting it with the significant moments of your life. Falling into bed after a long walk through unfamiliar streets, late-night talks filled with the thoughts and emotions of the day, planning the next breakfast at your newly favorite nearby boulangerie, sharing genuine smiles and the pure excitement of exploration.

A pied-à-terre always catches the irreplaceable light in your eyes, but it can also spark them. A flat in the city naturally blends you with those who live nearby. You are no longer a tourist, but someone who acts as if it were the most natural thing to buy lactose-free milk for your morning coffee it the shop downstairs.
And it is not a one-sided longing when we speak about pied-à-terre residences in Paris - the collection of architectural apartments in the prime location of The Moveable Feast.
/images/article-img-3.png
content.caption: Tuileries

content.content: Each of the apartments is a small journey within a larger one. When a space is carefully designed, you are respectfully offered so much to discover.

What feelings these walls evoke in you, how warm the textures are, how fluid the spatial organization seems, how gentle the rays of sun are in this flat, how loudly the floors creak, or perhaps the renovation has made them completely quiet. All these questions will never consciously arise in your mind, yet they will be answered silently within your body.

What I love about Pied-à-terre apartments is their complexity. The interiors are rich and confident.There is no attempt to reduce the experience of space to a minimum within your cognitive and emotional perception. Rather, the space helps you understand that sometimes valuable rest is not an introduction to emptiness, but a pleasant process of exploration That is exactly what we seek when booking flight tickets and anticipating the moment of landing in a distant place.

Being exposed to new memories with spiritual and material richness is nourishing for both soul and heart. I emphasize this duality because design is constructed from physical elements, yet everything within it contributes to the mental experience The perception of interconnections is more important than the mere existence of things.
In other words, we appreciate how the lily makes us feel inside, rather than the fact of its tangible existence.
/images/article-img-4.png
content.caption: Tuileries

content.content: /images/article-img-6.png
content.caption: Tuileries

content.content: Each apartment is exclusive, giving you the opportunity to choose what you want to experience.

The two-storey townhouse on the Tuileries by Necchi Architecture is a dichromatic sanctuary, gently enriched with details in a way that makes you feel calm. The elaborate visual density is created through the overlay and juxtaposition of forms. Shadows playing across the monochromatic volumes create a textured canvas for exquisite black and steel elements.

Chairs 212 by Petite Furniture recreate René Herbst's Sandows collection of 1929, standing beside the 1980s Cello lamp by Jan Wickelgren for IKEA. The integrated desk in front of the windows is accompanied by the Memmi lamp by Modenature.
The townhouse is not the only project designed by Charlotte Albert and Alexis Lamesta of Necchi Architecture. The Superior Loft on Oberkampf catches the eye from the very first second.
/images/article-img-7.png
content.caption: Oberkampf - Superior Loft

content.content: /images/article-img-8.png
content.caption: Oberkampf - Superior Loft

content.content: https://api.eprisjournal.com/uploads/mrmixdvyzqad6p.avif
content.caption: Oberkampf - Superior Loft

content.content:
Provocative patterns, red colour, chrome, and mirrors immediately transport us to a joyful evening in a 1970s Parisian apartment. The interior is composed of accents that support each other's flamboyance, grounded by touches of black.
You can almost see how the sunlight diffuses, moving from one corner of the room to another, hitting the lacquered surfaces and slipping past the sharp corners.
One of the most compelling qualities here is its unpredictability. You slightly turn your gaze and see a completely different scene, untouched by the perspective you just left.
/images/article-img-9.png
I have an association between a pied-a-terre and a box of exquisite chocolates.One carries orange zest, another lemon cream; the next reveals rosemary, while another holds delicate notes of jasmine.
The list continues, but once you know all the flavours, the spark of discovery is partly faded.
Explore it by yourself.

articles.title: Art that makes you smile
articles.author: EPRIS Journal
articles.excerpt: A focused opening paragraph for a new editorial story.

content.content: There is a preconception that pleasure derived from an art piece, or the smile it evokes, somehow lowers its value and strips it of the grand title of “Art.” However, this is simply not true.

Art can speak loudly without words, making it an indisputable instrument for materializing self-reflection or addressing social and political concerns. Yet, if we look at the etymology of the word, “art” is not historically related to “seriousness” or anything of the sort; it takes its roots in hand-related craft, brilliantly mastered and executed. This origin automatically deprives art of any mandatory obligations and legitimizes its right to just be.

Art serves as a reflection of social, political, gender, or other issues. It can be a medium for personal introspection or a vehicle to deliver complex ideas in diverse shapes and forms. But an artist still can create playful objects following an intrinsic desire to feel the material, to experiment with shapes and colors, to master techniques, and to bring a piece of joy to everyday life. And being playful is not synonymous with being superficial. All objects carry the background, beliefs, vision, intentions, and concerns of their creators - they are simply expressed through a different lens.

Taking everything too seriously is a dead end - and it certainly shouldn't cross your mind when you are choosing a new piece for your home. Ultimately, the baseline is your personal preference. If you only approve of strict beige and black forms, or conventional mediums and subjects, that is wonderful! My only concern is when people don't even allow themselves to consider a piece that might spark a moment of delight. We constantly hear: “It looks so great, it will go beautifully with my walls and my other paintings.” But next time, try asking yourself a different question: Does it make me smile? Colorful and playful objects possess a unique quality that others lack - they inject pure happiness into a room. We don’t think about this often enough, focusing instead on how trendy a piece is or how it makes our home look. But how does it make us feel?
Colorful and playful objects possess a unique quality that others lack - they inject pure happiness into a room.

Interior design is not just a setting, a static picture, a composition, or a color palette. It is a living environment that embraces and enswathes you. We are used to considering an interior as an external component of our existence, positioning ourselves merely as spectators. In reality, the moment we step into a space, we are absorbed by it, invisibly adopting its mood and yielding to the irresistible influence of its atmosphere. There is even a specific branch of science - neuroarchitecture - that studies how the environment affects our cognitive performance, as well as our psychological and physical well-being. Mostly, these effects are triggered by spatial characteristics, color psychology, and the tactile properties of materials. However, there is one element that often falls off this scientific list but still has a major impact on how we experience a space: details filled with irony and fun.
In reality, the moment we step into a space, we are absorbed by it, invisibly adopting its mood and yielding to the irresistible influence of its atmosphere.

I believe that when you introduce playful objects into an environment, it expands the boundaries of the mind. It gives you non-verbal permission to think freely - not just to push against conventional frames, but to forget they even exist.
Below, you will find a selection of artists whose objects are guaranteed to bring an unexpected smile to your face.
